sentences.text: Thousands and thousands.
Well, they were mostly Jewish.
But then, you see, Budapest, especially, had loads and loads of mixed marriages.
And neither party would leave the other one to go, so they marched together, and children and old people.
And they died on the way over.
And yet, he brought them home.
And one day, he was--
Raoul Wallenberg.
Well, with screaming-- how dare you taking the people?
These are all our protected people, and you are not to take it.
And I am going to go and tell-- and he came up with names, General so-and-so, that the German soldier had no idea what he's talking about.
And Germans always took orders.
So if this guy is screaming that I am going to tell, they let them go.
But he couldn't bring everybody home, because there were millions.
And he did this two, three times a week.
He had to do it.
And one day, he had a big, black book.
And it so  happened that that time, the first secretary [NON-ENGLISH] was with him.
And Raoul opened the book, and he was reading names.
And the people caught on, and whatever name he was calling, they came.
And on the way back, he turned to him, and he said, Raoul, I didn't know you have all the people's name.
And our Raoul starts hysterically laughing.
He said, yeah, I do.
Look.
And he opens the book.
It's nothing but cut pages-- not one name.
And then he went all the way to the train.
And the only way-- you see, the Hungarians are horrifying people, and the Arrow Cross was worse.
But when you gave them a drink, they could be changed, bought, bribing.
So Raoul usually had his car full of cognac and other kind of wine.
And he would tap the Hungarians.
He says, come here.
I have some wonderful drink for you.
And then we can get rid of the Jews.
And they drank so much, they were drunk like hell.
But then he said, go and open the big doors.
And you know, they were open.
And they did.
And so Raoul started to scream on those people.
Get out of there!
You are our people, and I have your papers.
And he brought out papers, driver's licenses, and you name it.
And people came out.
And again--
--he will take thousands home.
So he-- he would take them out of freight cars when they were on the way to being deported.
From the freight cars, on the way to be deported.
Back into the trucks.
He came with big Red Cross trucks.
But it happened many times that he-- he had those full.
And then he turned around, and he heard them killing the other people.
But you can't have the whole train saved, but he saved as many as possible.
No, he never took us to the train.
That we didn't.
But we had two things.
I first was a hostess.
And that means it was the word going around, and there is an angel.
And people dared to come there to ask.
And they had the pictures.
In the Swedish embassy, but was in the Buda side.
And it was dangerous for them, because they came with a star, and what not.
But somehow, they managed.
So Raoul-- we took the pictures.
And the other helper, they put the picture on.
And then the [NON-ENGLISH] was signing each of them.
And then we went out and gave it to the people.
And then Raoul would come out quickly, if he was there.
And he said, do you have a place to stay?
And they said, no.
He said, OK, you can come to our safe houses.
Now, the safe houses were not comfortable, because sometimes, in one apartment, two families lived.
But there was a Raoul Wallenberg and any of the other diplomats, who would come daily with medication, with food.
So those people were not hungry.
And as I said, those young kids who were outside taking care, they learned from Raoul.

And I remember, one afternoon, I-- I was in one of the houses just delivering some mail, I believe, when these two young men said that they just took people, old people, through the basement, and they're going after them.
And I said, how will you do it, kids?
He says, don't worry, Agnes, we'll come back.
Sure enough, what they did, they went after them.
And they spoke German beautiful.
And they screamed at these other Germans.
And he said, how dare are you!
We have to take them to-- and they came up with the name.
And these guys got frightened and said, and you have false papers.
Give me your identification.
And he took the real identification from the Germans.
And of course, the people didn't know who's who.
And they thought, now, we were killed from one or to the other.
And then the young man went up to, with the other three, to one of the old gentlemen.
He says, we are just taking you back to Raoul.
And they walked them right home.
So they dared, because they felt if Raoul can do it, we can do it.
They were fantastic, you know-- young kids of 16, 17 year old.
And they did it.
So for me, first, I was just a hostess.
Then I became sort of, what they called, a gopher, whatever they needed.
What did that involve?
Can you describe some experiences as a gopher?
As a gopher, yes.
For instance, one day-- we had a hospital already, a wonderful hospital.
In the very end, of course, anyone who was hurt on the street was bringing them there.
And before this hospital opened, Raoul decided to learn every single Arrow Cross officer's name and where they are located.
And it was a rather snowy day, and I had to deliver mail to all the patients and some medication, and to the doctors.
And I was the gopher to go there and bring it.
And Raoul arrived in the snow around 12:00 noon and found us all with our hands up.
And three young punks-- little Hungarian Arrow Cross kids, 12, 13, and 14 year old, with a gun.
And we had the Swedish flag outside.
So Hungarian, he didn't speak so much.
But he mixed it up, and he hit those three boys, and the guns fell down.
And he kicked it away.
He said, get out of here, and bring me your officer!
How dare you are to coming here to a Swedish house.
This is a Swedish territory.
So he was so loud and so bad, that these boys really got frightened.
They left.
About three minutes after, this Hungarian Arrow Cross officer walks in.
He knew who Raoul was.
And Raoul looks at the watch, and he said, Simon, what took you three minutes to get here?
And the man looks at, how do you know my name, Mr.
Wallenberg?
And Raoul says, I know everybody's name, and I really don't understand how dare you are to send three punks to frighten everybody in here.
I said, you could have killed everybody.
How dare you are?
I said, now, you better get out and bring me the medication, because you need help.
I don't need any help, Mr.
Wallenberg.
He said, oh boy, do you need help?
You know that you killed a Gestapo officer by mistake?
No, I didn't.
He said, oh yes, you did, because he died in our hospital.
And before he died, he sent your name up to the headquarters, and they're looking for you.
Simon left without a word.
And we all just stood there.
The doctor found his voice first.
And he said, Raoul, what did you just do?
And he starts laughing.
He says, I didn't do a thing.
Look, it's snowing outside, so it must have been next door.
And I knew that the next door's officer's name was Simon.
And we were lucky.
It was Simon.
So the doctor said, OK, it was Simon.
But I never had a Gestapo officer here.
So he starts laughing again.
He said, of course not.
But he killed so many people.
One of them could have been.
You see, this is how he saved the people.
And that's what gave us that strength, with me being a gopher.
Then the other gopher thing was Eichmann was after us all the time.
And Raoul invited him for dinner once, and he forgot.
So frantically, he called up three or four diplomats who had a very beautiful house, a villa.
And they had a housekeeper who was a great cook.
And he called up the Swiss litigation to send him food [LAUGHS] and invited Eichmann.
At that time, that city was bombed something awful.
And it was very near Christmas time, not yet in December, and-- beginning of September-- December.
And we had only candlelight.
And Raoul pulled the curtain away and said to Eichmann, look how the whole city is burning.
So get out of here.
You know that the Germans lost the war.
Stop killing the people.
Eichmann's answer was, no, I have a very good life.
And I know it might end any moment, but I'm going to do what I want.
And I will kill them all.
But you are  in my way, Mr.
Wallenberg.
So Raoul just looked at him.
He says, but not enough.
That's how he was, you know?
The next day, Eichmann's truck rammed into Raoul's car, but thanks God, he wasn't in.
And then he picked up the phone, and Eichmann, what did you do?
You are ruining my best Saab.
OK.
We also had an orphanage, where I constantly went to see that the children are all right.
And we took doctors.
And that's why you have to talk to Dr.
[NON-ENGLISH] because she was an orphan there, and she has a great story to tell.
And she and me are the speakers all the time.
So in any case, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the orphanage [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was-- the kids were afraid from bombing, and so was Raoul.
So what is he doing when the bombing starts?
He get out of the house.
He gets on his bicycle, and he bicycled to the children, because they needed a help, forgetting that he could be killed middle of the way.
And he picked up the  kids, and he started to sing to them and imitate animals.
So the kids started to laugh, and they forgot that there is a war.
That kind of a person he was.
So how could you not to be the same person, because you felt you have to do it?
This 32-year-old guy has no place to be here.
So then we had to constantly move, because Eichmann was after us.
And there was this gorgeous, gorgeous night.
A day before, the radio said that any young woman between the age of 15 and 25 has to go to such-and-such a sports palace to clean up Budapest.
But we knew that that means getting to Auschwitz and goodbye, not cleaning Budapest.
And Raoul turned to us all again, to [INAUDIBLE].
You all must have young friends.
Let's get the pictures.
So this time, as we had to move all the time, we were over at the Buda side, in a gorgeous villa.
And it was the most gorgeous night.
It was starry and beautiful moonlight out.
And I couldn't understand why they don't come and bombing us on a lovely night like this, you know?
And it was a curfew.
We were not allowed out, from 9:00 in the evening, till 7:00 in the morning.
So we were sitting there.
Again, they're saying they're doing it all.
And suddenly, Raoul arrived around 12:00-- noon-- 12:00 in the night.
And he said, are you all ready, because we have to deliver by 4:00?
And I said, I'm ready with 500.
And then he said, oh, I have to  tell you all a great news.
And we all looked up.
He says, don't look up.
Just work.
Only I want to tell you we have new neighbors-- the German headquarters.
And believe it or not, nobody was disturbed.
So the German headquarters, who cares?
And I went, and he gave me a big hug.
He says, good luck, and I hope you can deliver them all.
And I went around Budapest between, what was it, 1 o'clock and 4:00.
And in many houses, I rang the bell-- Jewish houses.
And out came a friend of mine.
Agie, what you doing out in the street?
I said, there is an angel who sent you a paper, and I hope you will live.
Goodbye.
And we kissed.
And I got home around 4 o'clock.
And I sat down on my bed suddenly.
And I said, Oh my God, what did I just do?
I could have been killed.
And then, like, Raoul would talk to me.
And he said, kill?
The Germans and the Hungarians wouldn't be dared to be out on a night like this.
And I went to sleep. 6 o'clock In the morning, the bell rings.
My two best friends-- the one I was in Berlin-- most were kicked out of this place because of that Swedish paper.
And then my father couldn't see these two girls crying from happiness.
And he said-- it was cold-- I go down and buy some bread.
And he put on his pajama, his coat on, and he never came back.
And we got the little note, suddenly.
I am in the Hungarian police station.
And as I said, there was big, big signs on the street.
This pass was showing.
We only find-- of course, I called up Raoul immediately.
It was 8 o'clock or 9 o'clock in the morning.
And he said, I'm just leaving for-- for the railway station, but I send you two diplomats.
He never said no to anything, you know, if he could help.
And sure enough, two diplomats arrived.
And we went all day long looking for Papa and couldn't find him.
So the next morning, they came at 6 o'clock already.
Said, let's go again.
Sure enough, we go.
And I see him going into one of these big places, right out of Pest, already.
And I see him saying, Heil Hitler, to this damn German there.
And he runs back to me.
He says, Papa is home.
Do we have a phone?
I said, yeah.
So we called home, and my father was home.
So then he told us the story.
He went down with his basket to buy the bread, and a Hungarian came up.
He says, now, you Jew, show me your papers.
So Papa pulled out very proudly the one what was on the wall.
He says, that's nothing.
No  good.
And he took him in.
A few minutes after, a German officer walked in.
And my father spoke German.
And he went up to him, and he said in German, look, the Hungarian took me.
And he was kicked out.
This went down eight times.
The Hungarian took him in.
The German took  him out.
Finally, the Hungarian won.
And it was a horribly cold morning, and here was Papa in pajama.
They walked them eight hours outside Budapest, on a big, big, big sports palace.
All night long, they  were standing there, with the bread still in his hand.
And in the morning, two German officers walked in, and they're telling jokes to each other.
And my father thought, this is the good time to go up.
And he went right out and said to him in German, look what the Hungarian did to me?
And he threw him out.
And there was a young man who also had the same Swedish paper, and he was thrown out.
And before Papa left, he gave the bread to an old gentleman.
They were standing there all night, and they were supposed to then get to Auschwitz.
So he got home, but he never left the house again.
Then, while we there in the apartment one day, the bell rings in the afternoon.
And I was still home or just home, back from the embassy.
And my parents came out, too.
And there stands a German soldier.
And first all of us were, whoops.
And then I looked again, and it turned out he is one German boy whom I met at  the Olympic games, with whom we changed cards.
And evidently, the name Mandl didn't tell him that I was a Jew.
But he wanted to see me.
So when I opened the door-- [LAUGHS] what else could I do?
I had to open the door.
Otherwise, maybe he would have broke it down.
And he says, Agie, can you see me?
He says, show me Budapest?
So I looked at my parents, and I said, I'll have to go.
And of course, I had no star.
And as we walked by the synagogue, this young chap stops.
He said, nah, that's where I'm going to kill every Jew.
So I thought to myself, now, what am I going to say?
If he finds out, he probably shoots me right here.
So I looked at him for a minute, and I said, well, why are you hating the Jews?
Did they ever hurt you?
He says, no, I never even met one.
I said, then why are you doing it?
He says, Hitler told me to do  it.
So at that point, I thought I'd better leave.
And I looked at my watch.
I said, oh, it was wonderful meeting you, but I think I have to run.
I have to give some English lessons and German lessons.
Goodbye.
And I went home.
I don't know what happened to him, and I couldn't care less.
But it was a frightening half an hour.
You see, they-- they didn't know what they're doing, but somebody told them kill.
He never even-- he said he never met a Jew.
OK, he didn't know I was one.
So we had all these little things.
And I also remember an old gentleman coming up one day at 3 o'clock to the embassy, while I was hostessing, too.
And he said, four, please.
And I said, Well, the ambassador is just leaving now, because 3 o'clock, the embassy had to be closed, and he had a girlfriend.
And he said, please, please, please, just four more.
So I went, and I said, please, Ambassador, just to sign your name is not a big deal.
So he said, OK, OK.
So he signed his name.
He put the pictures on, and the man came in quickly and signed.
Years passed, and who remembers the war?
Who cares?
I am sitting in a doctor's office here in New York.
A Hungarian man who came out, thanks God, before the war.
And his living room was his-- his place of-- waiting room.
Suddenly, down on the step comes an old gentleman.
And he starts screaming, you saved my life!
You saved my life.
And I looked around, because I forgot about the war already, you know.
This was in '52.
And he comes down and sits down and kisses my hand.
And I says, Uncle, what are you doing?
Then he told me this story, that he was the one who came up at  3 o'clock.
And I asked the ambassador, and all four of them survived.
And it was a funny feeling.
But it happened to Ambassador [NON-ENGLISH] too.
He was already a consul in Vienna, in 1956, when the uprising was from the other people.
And he figured, why don't I do what Raoul does?
I go down to the Danube-- because they came on the boat-- and I help people.
And they went down again with the Red Cross trucks.
And out comes a lady, and he sees-- we don't remember these people, but they remember us, you know.
And the lady starts running and says, Mr.
[NON-ENGLISH] you saved me from the Germans.
Now you're saving me from the Russians.
And it-- it was a great feeling.
Then, of course, the last big, big thing what happened was two weeks before Christmas.
That's why I had to stop working, because I got a little pneumonia.
Three days, Eichmann ordered the Hungarian Nazis to kill the Jews into the Danube, because they will die.
It was frozen.
In 40 years, it was never that cold in Hungary.
And there were big icicles in there.
They roped three together, and they shot the middle one.
So naturally, all three fell in.
Some of them got out by themselves, but very few.
So the third day, Raoul was out saving people somewhere else.
And he said, what will Eichmann say next?
How many of you can swim?
And I was the only one who put up my hand.
He says, let's go.
I had no idea where we're going.
It was a very dark, starless night.
And we went with the Red Cross trucks-- three diplomats, and myself.
And we went down.
They didn't even know that we came.
You know, we turned off the lights, and there was no noise.
And we synchronized.
Every time he shot, we jumped.
And thanks to the icicles-- they were-- the rope what-- what-- got stuck to it.
So we knew where there is a rope, there is a body, and we pulled the body out.
They didn't hear us.
We heard the shooting, yes, but they didn't hear us coming, thanks God.
The Germans-- the Hungarian Nazis didn't hear the trucks coming, because they were too busy roping and shooting.
Oh, I understand thousands and thousands.
I don't know.
Oh, yes.
We heard everything.
But we had to also listen.
When he shoots, we have to jump.
And we saved 50 people.
Swam to the icicle.
We just jumped.
Fur coats, boots.
With these three diplomats.
And we saved 50 people.
This I can't even-- we were strong.
You get such a strengths when you have to.
And some of these people knew how to swim, you know.
And so they knew.
And we said, we are saving you.
Come, come.
And we pulled.
And they'd-- they'd-- they'd go on-- they were going on shooting, so they didn't hear us bringing these people out. 50 people we saved there.
Four of us.
On the side.
And then the Red Cross people and the doctors came and put them in, and  all that.
And then we went to the hospital, and Raoul was waiting for us.
And he didn't ask how many we saved.
The first question was, did you save any young?
And this was the only time I ever could talk to him, because we never had time to talk.
And I said, Raoul, all your talk, all your life is the young, the young, because the young haven't had a life.
What do you want me to do?
I am fairly young still.
I was already about, I think, 26, 25.
But I was still young, [LAUGHS] you know, and I wanted to live.
And he said, oh, it's so easy.
Prejudice has to disappear.
He said, I don't know colors.
I don't know religion.
I only know two kind of people, that is the good and the bad.
And if you can make a people good, then that's what you have to do.
And I got pneumonia.
Almost three days after.
Oh, yes, because the 50 people we had to save out.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so we-- we jumped in there.
And then I-- at first, I got dizzy and all that.
And my super, he hasn't finished with me yet, as you remember that.
So I got sick, and we needed a doctor.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
The super said, I have one.
And that doctor came, and he gave me me, with a needle like this, a sulfa.
And in about half an hour after that, I was unconscious.
I knew nothing.
So here we were, desperate that I'm going to die, because I knew nothing about myself anymore.
So my-- my mother went and called a friend.
We had a telephone still, and that worked.
And she found a Jewish doctor who was somewhere hiding.
And he came to the house and asked my mother what they did.
And Mommy said, with a needle like this.
And my God, that's for animals.
We don't give a needle like this sulfa.
So they found something.
And he said, this child has to get well, and even in a wheelchair, she has to get on a train and get out of here.
Anyhow, we told him then that he should work for Raoul, because he needs other doctors.
And I survived, but I could not get out of the house, because it took me quite a while.
So I only spoke to Raoul on the phone.
